question_en: In what year did World War I end?
context_en: World War I ended on 11 November 1918 with the signing of the Armistice of Compiègne. The conflict caused more than 20 million deaths.

question_en: Who was the first emperor of Rome?
context_en: Augustus (Octavian) was the first Roman emperor, ruling from 27 BC to AD 14. Julius Caesar was never officially 'emperor'.

question_en: In what year did the Berlin Wall fall?
context_en: The Berlin Wall fell on 9 November 1989, a symbol of the end of the Cold War and the start of German reunification.

question_en: Which civilization built Machu Picchu?
context_en: Machu Picchu was built by the Inca Empire in the 15th century as a residence for emperor Pachacútec, at 2,430 m of altitude.

question_en: In what year did Christopher Columbus reach America?
context_en: On 12 October 1492, Columbus reached Guanahaní (Bahamas) believing he had reached Asia. It marked the start of the colonial era.

question_en: In what year did World War II end?
context_en: World War II ended in 1945: in Europe on 8 May (V-E Day) and in the Pacific on 2 September, after Japan's surrender.

question_en: Who was the first president of the United States?
context_en: George Washington was the first U.S. president (1789-1797). He was the military leader during the American Revolution.

question_en: In what year did the French Revolution begin?
context_en: The French Revolution began in 1789 with the storming of the Bastille on 14 July. It proclaimed the ideals of liberty, equality and fraternity.

question_en: Who was Napoleon Bonaparte?
options_en: A general with no political office
context_en: Napoleon was Emperor of the French (1804-1815). He conquered much of Europe before being defeated at Waterloo.

question_en: In which battle was Napoleon definitively defeated?
context_en: At Waterloo (1815), the allied forces of Wellington and Blücher defeated Napoleon, who was exiled to Saint Helena where he died in 1821.

question_en: Which empire was known as 'the empire on which the sun never sets'?
context_en: The British Empire at its peak (19th century) covered 24% of the Earth's land. The phrase was earlier attributed to the Spanish Empire.

question_en: Who was Cleopatra?
context_en: Cleopatra VII was the last active queen of Ancient Egypt (69-30 BC). She had romances with Julius Caesar and Mark Antony. She spoke 9 languages.

question_en: In what year was the Magna Carta signed in England?
context_en: The Magna Carta was signed in 1215 by King John of England, limiting royal power and laying the foundations of modern constitutionalism.

question_en: Which civilization built the Pyramids of Giza?
context_en: The Pyramids of Giza were built by Ancient Egypt, roughly between 2560 and 2490 BC. The Great Pyramid is that of Khufu.

question_en: Which was the first city in history?
context_en: Uruk (in present-day Iraq) is considered the first city in history, with ~50,000 inhabitants around 3200 BC. Birthplace of cuneiform writing.

question_en: Which war pitted Athens against Sparta?
context_en: The Peloponnesian War (431-404 BC) pitted the Delian League (Athens) against the Peloponnesian League (Sparta). Sparta won.

question_en: In what year did the Western Roman Empire fall?
context_en: The Western Roman Empire fell in AD 476 when Odoacer deposed the last emperor, Romulus Augustulus.

question_en: Who was the conqueror of the Aztec Empire?
context_en: Hernán Cortés conquered the Aztec Empire between 1519-1521, aided by alliances with rival peoples and the smallpox that decimated the population.

question_en: Which event triggered World War I?
options_en: The assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand
context_en: The assassination of Austro-Hungarian archduke Franz Ferdinand in Sarajevo (28 June 1914) triggered the chain of alliances that led to war.

question_en: What was the Spanish Inquisition?
context_en: The Spanish Inquisition (1478-1834) was an ecclesiastical tribunal created by the Catholic Monarchs to maintain Catholic orthodoxy.

question_en: In what year did the Russian Revolution take place?
context_en: The 1917 Russian Revolution had two phases: the February Revolution (fall of the tsar) and October (the Bolshevik seizure of power).

question_en: Who was Adolf Hitler?
context_en: Hitler was chancellor from 1933 and proclaimed himself Führer in 1934. He led Nazism, causing the Holocaust and World War II.

question_en: What was Apartheid?
options_en: A system of racial segregation in South Africa
context_en: Apartheid was a regime of racial segregation in South Africa (1948-1991). Nelson Mandela fought against it and was imprisoned for 27 years.

question_en: In what year did man reach the Moon?
context_en: On 20 July 1969, Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin (Apollo 11) walked on the Moon. Armstrong was the first, saying 'one small step for man'.

question_en: Which civil war took place in Spain between 1936 and 1939?
context_en: The Spanish Civil War (1936-1939) pitted the Republican side against the Nationalist side (Franco). It ended with Franco's dictatorship, which lasted until 1975.

question_en: Who was Marco Polo?
options_en: An Italian explorer who traveled to China
context_en: Marco Polo (1254-1324) was a Venetian merchant and explorer who traveled to the court of the Great Khan in China and described his journeys in 'The Travels'.

question_en: What was the Spanish Reconquista?
options_en: The recovery of Iberian territories occupied by Muslims
The union of Castile and Aragon
context_en: The Reconquista (711-1492) was the process by which the Christian kingdoms recovered the Iberian Peninsula from the Muslims. It ended with the taking of Granada.

question_en: Which was the first country to grant women the vote?
context_en: New Zealand was the first country in the world to grant women's suffrage in 1893. British women got it in 1918 and French women in 1944.

question_en: What was the Holocaust?
options_en: The systematic genocide of Jews and other groups by the Nazis
context_en: The Holocaust was the systematic genocide of 6 million Jews and millions of others by the Nazi regime between 1941 and 1945.

question_en: Who was Genghis Khan?
options_en: The founder of the Mongol Empire
context_en: Genghis Khan (1162-1227) founded the Mongol Empire, the largest contiguous empire in history. He conquered from China to Eastern Europe.

question_en: Which was the largest empire in history?
context_en: The Mongol Empire (13th century) reached 24 million km², the largest contiguous empire in history. The British Empire was the largest by total area, at 35.5 million km².

question_en: Who was Julius Caesar?
options_en: A Roman general and dictator murdered in the Senate
context_en: Julius Caesar was a Roman dictator murdered on 15 March 44 BC (the 'Ides of March'). His great-nephew Augustus became the first emperor.

question_en: What was the Black Death?
options_en: A bubonic plague epidemic that killed a third of Europe
context_en: The Black Death (1347-1353) killed between 30 and 60% of Europe's population, some 25 million people. It was caused by the bacterium Yersinia pestis.

question_en: What was the Hundred Years' War?
options_en: A war between Rome and Carthage
A conflict between England and France (1337-1453)
context_en: The Hundred Years' War (1337-1453) pitted England against France over the French throne. Joan of Arc was the French heroine who turned the tide of the war.

question_en: Who was Joan of Arc?
options_en: A French peasant who led armies and was burned as a heretic
context_en: Joan of Arc (1412-1431) led the French army guided by religious visions. She was captured, tried for heresy and burned alive. Canonized in 1920.

question_en: What was the Renaissance?
options_en: A cultural and artistic movement that revived classical Greco-Roman culture
context_en: The Renaissance (14th-17th centuries) began in Italy and spread across Europe. It revalued humanism, art and science. Leonardo, Michelangelo and Erasmus are its figures.

question_en: What was the Protestant Reformation?
options_en: The religious movement started by Luther that split Christianity
The reform of the Catholic Church at the Council of Trent
context_en: Martin Luther began the Reformation in 1517 by publishing his 95 theses. It created Protestantism, dividing European Christianity into Catholics and Protestants.

question_en: Who was Martin Luther?
options_en: A monk and theologian who started the Protestant Reformation
context_en: Martin Luther (1483-1546) was an Augustinian monk who in 1517 published his 95 theses against indulgences. He was excommunicated but protected by German princes.

question_en: What was the Spanish Armada?
options_en: The English fleet that defeated Spain
The Spanish fleet sent to invade England in 1588, which was defeated
context_en: Philip II sent the Spanish Armada in 1588 to invade England. It was defeated by Drake's English fleet and by storms. It marked Spain's decline.

question_en: Who was Galileo Galilei?
options_en: An astronomer and physicist who defended heliocentrism
A doctor who discovered blood circulation
context_en: Galileo (1564-1642) improved the telescope, observed Jupiter's satellites and supported Copernicus's heliocentrism. He was condemned by the Inquisition.

question_en: What was the Industrial Revolution?
options_en: A political revolution in Great Britain
The process of mechanizing production that began in Great Britain in the 18th century
A technological revolution in the U.S.
context_en: The Industrial Revolution began in Great Britain around 1760 with Watt's steam engine. It transformed the world economy from agricultural to industrial.

question_en: Who invented the movable-type printing press in Europe?
context_en: Gutenberg invented the movable-type printing press in Europe around 1450, printing the Gutenberg Bible. It revolutionized the spread of knowledge.

question_en: What was the Inquisition?
options_en: An ecclesiastical tribunal to persecute heresy
context_en: The Inquisition was an ecclesiastical tribunal that persecuted heresy in Europe from the 12th century. The Spanish one (1478-1834) was especially rigorous.

question_en: When did the United States become independent?
context_en: The Declaration of Independence was on 4 July 1776. The war ended in 1783 with the Treaty of Paris. The Constitution came into force in 1789.

question_en: Who was Simón Bolívar?
options_en: The hero of Latin American independence who liberated Venezuela, Colombia, Ecuador, Peru and Bolivia
context_en: Simón Bolívar (1783-1830) led the independence of Venezuela, Colombia, Ecuador, Peru and Bolivia, which bears his name. He is known as 'The Liberator'.

question_en: What was the Crimean War?
options_en: A 19th-century war between Russia and Turkey
A medieval war between Mongols and Russians
context_en: The Crimean War (1853-1856) pitted Russia against the Ottoman Empire, France, the UK and Sardinia. Florence Nightingale modernized nursing during this conflict.

question_en: Who was Nelson Mandela?
options_en: Activist and first black president of South Africa
Leader of the civil rights movement in the U.S.
context_en: Nelson Mandela (1918-2013) fought against Apartheid, was imprisoned for 27 years and became the first democratically elected president of South Africa (1994).

question_en: What was the Cold War?
options_en: A war between the Arctic and Antarctica
The political and military tension between the U.S. and the USSR (1947-1991)
An undeclared war between Europe and Russia
context_en: The Cold War (1947-1991) was the rivalry between the U.S. and the USSR without direct conflict. It included the arms race, the space race and proxy conflicts.

question_en: In what year was the Soviet Union dissolved?
context_en: The USSR was officially dissolved on 25 December 1991, when Gorbachev resigned. It gave rise to 15 independent republics, with Russia as the main successor.

question_en: Who was Mao Zedong?
options_en: The first president of the Republic of China
The founder of the People's Republic of China and leader of the Chinese Communist Party
context_en: Mao Zedong (1893-1976) founded the People's Republic of China in 1949. His 'Great Leap Forward' and the Cultural Revolution caused tens of millions of deaths.

question_en: What was Pearl Harbor?
options_en: The Japanese attack on the U.S. naval base in Hawaii in 1941
A naval battle in the Pacific
context_en: On 7 December 1941, Japan attacked the base at Pearl Harbor. It brought the U.S. into World War II. Roosevelt called it 'a date which will live in infamy'.

question_en: Who was Hernán Cortés?
options_en: The conqueror of the Inca Empire
The conqueror of the Aztec Empire
context_en: Hernán Cortés (1485-1547) conquered the Aztec Empire between 1519-1521 with 600 men, relying on alliances with peoples hostile to the Aztecs.

question_en: What was the Battle of Lepanto (1571)?
options_en: A battle between Spain and Portugal
The victory of the Holy League over the Ottoman Turks in the Mediterranean
context_en: At Lepanto (1571) the Holy League (Spain, Venice, the Pope) defeated the Ottoman fleet. Miguel de Cervantes took part and lost the use of his left hand.

question_en: What was the Marshall Plan?
options_en: The U.S. economic aid program to rebuild Europe after WWII
A peace treaty between Germany and the Allies
A military strategy to contain the USSR
context_en: The Marshall Plan (1948-1952) was the U.S. economic aid program that provided $12 billion to rebuild Western Europe.

question_en: When was the Cuban Revolution?
context_en: The Cuban Revolution triumphed on 1 January 1959, when Fidel Castro overthrew dictator Batista. Fidel ruled Cuba until 2008, when he handed power to his brother Raúl.

question_en: What was the Cuban Missile Crisis (1962)?
options_en: The nuclear confrontation between the U.S. and the USSR over Soviet missiles in Cuba
A war between Cuba and the U.S.
context_en: In 1962, the U.S. discovered Soviet missiles in Cuba. The 13 days of tension between Kennedy and Khrushchev were the closest the world came to nuclear war.

question_en: What was the Battle of Waterloo?
options_en: Napoleon's final defeat by Wellington and Blücher in 1815
The French victory in the Russian campaign
context_en: At Waterloo (18 June 1815), Napoleon was defeated by the coalition of Wellington (UK) and Blücher (Prussia). He was exiled to Saint Helena.

question_en: In what year was the UN founded?
context_en: The UN was founded on 24 October 1945, with 51 founding countries, to maintain peace after World War II. Today it has 193 member states.

question_en: Who was Leonardo da Vinci?
options_en: A Renaissance genius: painter, sculptor, architect, scientist and inventor
context_en: Leonardo da Vinci (1452-1519) was the archetype of the 'Renaissance man'. He painted the Mona Lisa and The Last Supper, designed flying machines and studied anatomy.

question_en: What was the Vietnam War?
options_en: A war between North and South Vietnam, with U.S. intervention
A war of independence against Japan
context_en: The Vietnam War (1955-1975) pitted communist North Vietnam (backed by the USSR and China) against the South (backed by the U.S.). It ended with communist unification.

question_en: In what year was the Treaty of Versailles signed?
context_en: The Treaty of Versailles was signed on 28 June 1919, formally ending World War I and imposing harsh conditions on Germany.

question_en: Who was the last tsar of Russia?
context_en: Nicholas II Romanov ruled from 1894 to 1917. He was executed along with his family in 1918 after the Bolshevik Revolution.

question_en: Which Roman emperor legalized Christianity?
context_en: Constantine I issued the Edict of Milan in the year 313, which granted religious freedom and legalized Christianity in the Empire.

question_en: In which century did Charlemagne live?
context_en: Charlemagne (742-814) was king of the Franks and emperor of the Romans. He was crowned by Pope Leo III in the year 800.

question_en: Which Chinese dynasty built most of the current Great Wall?
context_en: The Ming dynasty (1368-1644) rebuilt and expanded most of the sections of the Great Wall of China visible today.

question_en: Who was assassinated in Sarajevo in 1914?
context_en: The assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria by Gavrilo Princip on 28 June 1914 triggered World War I.

question_en: Which treaty divided the New World between Spain and Portugal?
context_en: The Treaty of Tordesillas (1494) drew a line of demarcation dividing the discovered and yet-to-be-discovered lands between Castile and Portugal.

question_en: Which event conventionally marks the start of the Modern Age?
context_en: The discovery of America in 1492 (along with the fall of Constantinople in 1453) is usually cited as the start of the Modern Age.

question_en: In what year did Constantinople fall?
context_en: Constantinople fell to the Ottomans of Mehmed II on 29 May 1453, marking the end of the Byzantine Empire.

question_en: Which pharaoh signed the first known peace treaty?
context_en: Ramesses II signed the Treaty of Kadesh with the Hittites around 1259 BC, considered the first written peace treaty in history.

question_en: What was the Bauhaus?
context_en: The Bauhaus was a German school of art, design and architecture founded by Walter Gropius in Weimar in 1919; closed by the Nazis in 1933.

question_en: Which empire did Mehmed II establish after the capture of Constantinople?
context_en: Mehmed II 'the Conqueror' consolidated the Ottoman Empire and made Constantinople (renamed Istanbul) its capital.

question_en: Which U.S. document was signed on 4 July 1776?
context_en: The Declaration of Independence, drafted mainly by Thomas Jefferson, proclaimed the independence of the Thirteen Colonies from Great Britain.

question_en: Who was the 'Liberator' of Argentina, Chile and Peru?
context_en: José de San Martín (1778-1850) led the military campaigns that freed Argentina, Chile and part of Peru from Spanish rule.

question_en: Which war pitted the North and South of the United States (1861-1865)?
context_en: The Civil War pitted the Union (North) against the Confederate States (South). It ended with a Northern victory and the abolition of slavery.

question_en: Who was Abraham Lincoln?
context_en: Abraham Lincoln was the 16th president of the U.S. (1861-1865). He abolished slavery with the Emancipation Proclamation and was assassinated in 1865.

question_en: In what year was the current Spanish Constitution enacted?
context_en: The Spanish Constitution was enacted on 27 December 1978 after a referendum, consolidating the transition to democracy.

question_en: Who was the dictator of Spain between 1939 and 1975?
context_en: Francisco Franco ruled Spain as a dictator from the end of the Civil War (1939) until his death in 1975.

question_en: Which empire dominated Mesoamerica before the arrival of the Spanish?
context_en: The Aztec (Mexica) Empire dominated central Mexico with its capital at Tenochtitlan until its fall in 1521 to Hernán Cortés.

question_en: What was the French May of 1968?
context_en: In May 1968, students and workers led massive protests in France that challenged De Gaulle's government and the established social order.

question_en: Which pre-Columbian empire had its capital at Cuzco?
context_en: Cuzco was the capital of the Inca Empire, considered by the Incas the 'navel of the world' (Qosqo).

question_en: Who was Mahatma Gandhi?
context_en: Gandhi (1869-1948) led India's independence from the United Kingdom through nonviolent resistance (satyagraha).

question_en: In what year did India become independent?
context_en: India became independent from the British Empire on 15 August 1947. The same process gave rise to Pakistan.

question_en: Which war pitted North Korea against South Korea (1950-1953)?
context_en: The Korean War (1950-1953) pitted the communist North (backed by China and the USSR) against the South (backed by the UN and the U.S.).

question_en: What was the 'Crash' of 1929?
options_en: A stock market crisis in the U.S.
context_en: The Wall Street Crash of October 1929 marked the start of the Great Depression, the largest economic crisis of the 20th century.

question_en: Who delivered the 'I have a dream' speech?
context_en: Martin Luther King Jr. delivered his famous speech on 28 August 1963 during the March on Washington for civil rights.

question_en: In what year was John F. Kennedy assassinated?
context_en: JFK was assassinated in Dallas on 22 November 1963. The alleged perpetrator, Lee Harvey Oswald, was himself killed two days later.

question_en: What was the Maastricht Treaty (1992)?
context_en: The Maastricht Treaty, signed in 1992 and in force in 1993, created the European Union and laid the foundations for the euro.

question_en: In what year did the euro enter circulation as physical currency?
context_en: The euro entered physical circulation on 1 January 2002 in twelve EU countries, although it had existed as an accounting currency since 1999.

question_en: Which empire did Philip II of Spain rule?
context_en: Philip II (1556-1598) ruled an empire on which 'the sun never set', spanning territories in Europe, America, Asia and Africa.

question_en: Who defeated the Persians at the Battle of Marathon (490 BC)?
context_en: Athens, led by Miltiades, defeated the troops of Darius I at Marathon. Pheidippides ran 42 km to announce the victory.

question_en: Which emperor built the Colosseum in Rome?
context_en: Emperor Vespasian began construction of the Flavian Amphitheatre (Colosseum) in AD 72; his son Titus inaugurated it in AD 80.

question_en: Who was Alexander the Great?
context_en: Alexander III of Macedon (356-323 BC) conquered a vast empire stretching from Greece to India. He was a pupil of Aristotle.

question_en: Which 20th-century war pitted the U.S. against a Southeast Asian country and lasted ~20 years?
context_en: The Vietnam War (1955-1975) ended with the fall of Saigon and unification under the communist regime of the North.

question_en: What was the 'Prague Spring' (1968)?
options_en: An attempt at reform in Czechoslovakia
context_en: The Prague Spring was a period of political liberalization in Czechoslovakia, crushed by the invasion of the Warsaw Pact in August 1968.

question_en: Which year marked the start of the Industrial Revolution in England (approx.)?
context_en: The Industrial Revolution began around 1760-1780 in Great Britain, with the mechanization of textiles and the steam engine.

question_en: Which empire did Suleiman the Magnificent rule?
context_en: Suleiman I 'the Magnificent' (1520-1566) brought the Ottoman Empire to its peak, expanding across the Balkans, North Africa and the Middle East.

question_en: What was Mao's 'Long March'?
options_en: A strategic retreat of the CCP
context_en: The Long March (1934-1935) was the retreat of the Chinese Red Army, covering ~10,000 km. It consolidated Mao Zedong's leadership.

question_en: Who was Charles V?
context_en: Charles V (Charles I of Spain) was Holy Roman Emperor (1519-1556). He inherited an immense empire.

question_en: What name is given to the U.S. anti-communist doctrine after World War II?
context_en: The Truman Doctrine (1947) declared that the U.S. would help countries threatened by communism, formally beginning the Cold War.

question_en: What was the Enlightenment?
context_en: The Enlightenment was an 18th-century movement that championed reason, science and individual rights. It influenced the French Revolution.

question_en: Which 20th-century civil war inspired Hemingway's 'For Whom the Bell Tolls'?
context_en: Ernest Hemingway's novel is set in the Spanish Civil War (1936-1939), in which he took part as a correspondent.

question_en: What was 'D-Day'?
context_en: 'D-Day' was the Allied landing in Normandy on 6 June 1944, one of the largest military operations in history.

question_en: Which bomb did the U.S. drop on Hiroshima in 1945?
context_en: 'Little Boy' was the uranium atomic bomb dropped on Hiroshima on 6 August 1945. 'Fat Man' fell on Nagasaki three days later.

question_en: Who was Winston Churchill?
context_en: Churchill was prime minister of the United Kingdom (1940-1945 and 1951-1955), the British leader during World War II. Nobel laureate in Literature 1953.

question_en: What was Thailand called before 1939?
context_en: Thailand was officially called 'Siam' until 1939. It is one of the few Southeast Asian countries never to be colonized.

question_en: In what year was Germany reunified?
context_en: Germany was officially reunified on 3 October 1990, almost a year after the fall of the Berlin Wall.

question_en: Who was Joseph Stalin?
context_en: Stalin led the USSR from the mid-1920s until his death in 1953. He industrialized the country at the cost of millions of victims.

question_en: What was the GULAG?
context_en: The Gulag was the Soviet system of forced-labor camps where millions of people were imprisoned during the Stalinist regime.

question_en: In what year did the war in Ukraine begin (the full-scale Russian invasion)?
context_en: Russia launched the full-scale invasion of Ukraine on 24 February 2022. The annexation of Crimea had occurred in 2014.

question_en: Which power launched the Crusades in 1095?
context_en: Pope Urban II called for the First Crusade at the Council of Clermont (1095), beginning the campaigns to reconquer the Holy Land.

question_en: Who was Saladin?
context_en: Saladin (Salah ad-Din, 1137-1193) was sultan of Egypt and Syria. He recaptured Jerusalem for Islam in 1187.

question_en: What role did Eva Perón play?
context_en: Eva Duarte de Perón ('Evita', 1919-1952) was First Lady of Argentina and an icon of Peronism, a champion of social rights and women's rights.

question_en: In what year was Lincoln assassinated?
context_en: Abraham Lincoln was assassinated by John Wilkes Booth on 14 April 1865, a few days after the end of the Civil War.

question_en: Which Egyptian dynasty did Tutankhamun belong to?
context_en: Tutankhamun belonged to the 18th dynasty of the Egyptian New Kingdom (14th century BC). His tomb was discovered by Howard Carter in 1922.

question_en: Which Roman emperor formally divided the empire into East and West?
context_en: Theodosius I 'the Great' divided the Roman Empire between his sons Arcadius and Honorius in AD 395, a partition that became permanent.

question_en: What was the Battle of Trafalgar (1805)?
context_en: At Trafalgar (Cádiz), Nelson's British fleet defeated the Franco-Spanish fleet. Nelson died in combat.

question_en: Which dynasty currently rules Spain?
context_en: The House of Bourbon has ruled Spain since 1700 (with interruptions). The current king is Felipe VI since 2014.

question_en: Who first unified China (221 BC)?
context_en: Qin Shi Huang was the first emperor of a unified China and ordered the construction of much of the Great Wall and his famous terracotta army.

question_en: What was the 'Pax Romana'?
context_en: The Pax Romana was a period of relative peace and stability in the Roman Empire, roughly from 27 BC to AD 180.

question_en: Which English document of 1215 limited the power of the king?
context_en: The Magna Carta was imposed on King John by the barons in 1215; a milestone in limiting royal power and the development of constitutionalism.

question_en: In what year was the attack on the Twin Towers?
context_en: On 11 September 2001, 19 Al-Qaeda terrorists attacked the WTC and the Pentagon, causing nearly 3,000 deaths.

question_en: Who was the first man to circumnavigate the globe (his expedition achieved it)?
context_en: Ferdinand Magellan began the expedition; he died in the Philippines (1521). Juan Sebastián Elcano completed the voyage in 1522.

question_en: What caused the final fall of the Byzantine Empire?
context_en: The Byzantine Empire fell when the Ottoman Turks took Constantinople in 1453, under the command of Mehmed II.

question_en: Who was the 'Father of the Nation' in Italy after unification (1861)?
context_en: Victor Emmanuel II was proclaimed king of Italy in 1861. Garibaldi and Cavour were decisive figures in the unification process.

question_en: What was the Thirty Years' War (1618-1648)?
context_en: It began as a religious conflict (Catholics vs. Protestants) in the Holy Roman Empire and ended with the Peace of Westphalia, the basis of the modern state system.

question_en: Which Chinese emperor had his terracotta army built?
context_en: The first emperor, Qin Shi Huang, ordered the construction of the terracotta army (~8,000 figures) to protect him in the afterlife.

question_en: Who led the Cuban Revolution in 1959?
context_en: Fidel Castro overthrew Fulgencio Batista on 1 January 1959 and ruled Cuba for almost five decades.

question_en: What was the Battle of Stalingrad (1942-1943)?
options_en: A turning point in World War II
context_en: It was one of the bloodiest battles in history. The German defeat by the USSR marked the turning of the war on the Eastern Front.

question_en: In what year was NATO founded?
context_en: NATO was founded on 4 April 1949 through the Treaty of Washington, as a military alliance against the USSR.

question_en: When was the Warsaw Pact signed?
context_en: The Warsaw Pact was signed in 1955 between the USSR and its Eastern European allies, in response to NATO.

question_en: Which empire destroyed Carthage in 146 BC?
context_en: Rome destroyed Carthage at the end of the Third Punic War (146 BC). General Scipio Aemilianus razed the city.

question_en: Who was Hannibal Barca?
context_en: Hannibal (247-183 BC) was the Carthaginian general who crossed the Alps with elephants during the Second Punic War to attack Rome.

question_en: Which emperor made Christianity the official religion of the Roman Empire?
context_en: Theodosius I issued the Edict of Thessalonica (AD 380) which established Nicene Christianity as the official religion of the Empire.

question_en: What was the Paris Commune (1871)?
context_en: The Commune was a popular revolutionary government that controlled Paris from March to May 1871, bloodily repressed by the French government.

question_en: Which European empire colonized Brazil?
context_en: Brazil was a Portuguese colony from 1500 until its independence in 1822. That is why it is the largest Portuguese-speaking country in the world.

question_en: When was the Universal Declaration of Human Rights signed?
context_en: It was proclaimed by the UN General Assembly on 10 December 1948 in Paris, drafted by a commission chaired by Eleanor Roosevelt.

question_en: Which empire ruled Persia before Alexander the Great?
context_en: The Achaemenid Empire (550-330 BC) was defeated by Alexander the Great. Its most famous kings were Cyrus, Darius I and Xerxes.

question_en: Which city was the cradle of the Renaissance?
context_en: Florence, under the patronage of the Medici, was the main artistic, literary and philosophical hub of the Renaissance in the 15th century.

question_en: Who was Lorenzo de' Medici?
context_en: Lorenzo 'the Magnificent' (1449-1492) was the de facto ruler of Florence and a great patron of Botticelli, Michelangelo and Leonardo.

question_en: Which empire dominated India between the 16th and 19th centuries?
context_en: The Mughal Empire (1526-1857) ruled much of the Indian subcontinent. Its most famous emperor, Akbar, was tolerant of all religions.

question_en: Who ordered the Taj Mahal to be built?
context_en: Shah Jahan ordered the Taj Mahal to be built (1632-1653) in Agra as a mausoleum for his wife Mumtaz Mahal.

question_en: What was the Tudor dynasty?
context_en: The Tudors reigned in England from Henry VII to Elizabeth I. They include Henry VIII and his six wives.

question_en: Who was Elizabeth I of England?
context_en: Elizabeth I (1558-1603), daughter of Henry VIII and Anne Boleyn, was known as 'the Virgin Queen'. Her reign was a cultural and naval 'golden age'.

question_en: What was the Boxer Rebellion (1899-1901)?
options_en: An armed conflict in the U.S.
context_en: It was a nationalist revolt in China against foreign and missionary influence. It was crushed by an international coalition.

question_en: When was the Red Cross founded?
context_en: The Red Cross was founded in Geneva in 1863 by Henry Dunant after witnessing the Battle of Solferino. It earned him the first Nobel Peace Prize (1901).

question_en: Which empire dominated the region of Egypt in the 7th century BC?
context_en: The Assyrian Empire conquered Egypt in the 7th century BC under Esarhaddon and Ashurbanipal, before its own fall in 612 BC.

question_en: Which treaty ended the Thirty Years' War (1648)?
context_en: The Peace of Westphalia (1648) ended the Thirty Years' War and laid the foundations of the modern nation-state system.
